title: Current Python SDK Surface
summary: Current Microsoft Foundry quickstarts use azure-ai-projects version 2.x, AIProjectClient, project.agents.create version, PromptAgentDefinition, project.get openai client, conversations, and responses with agent reference.
analogy: Imagine two radio manuals on the desk: the new manual says use the digital mission console, while the old manual says operate individual channel switches. Both describe agents, but they are not interchangeable procedures.
diagram:
graph TD
New[Azure AI Projects 2.x] --> CreateVersion[project agents create version]
CreateVersion --> Responses[OpenAI responses with agent reference]
Classic[Azure AI Agents samples] --> CreateAgent[agents client create agent]
CreateAgent --> ThreadRun[threads messages runs run steps]
body:
## First principles

First principles: SDK names are contracts between your code and a cloud API. When a service evolves, the safest engineer separates current GA paths from older or preview paths instead of mixing snippets blindly.

## Definition

Current Microsoft Foundry quickstarts use azure-ai-projects version 2.x, AIProjectClient, project.agents.create_version, PromptAgentDefinition, project.get_openai_client, conversations, and responses with agent_reference. The older classic persistent-agent flow uses azure-ai-agents objects such as agents_client.create_agent, threads, messages, runs, and run_steps and is still visible in SDK samples.

## How it fits together

For new Foundry projects, start with azure-ai-projects >= 2.0.0. Use azure-ai-agents samples only when you intentionally need the classic or persistent thread-run API shape shown by that package. Treat MCP examples as moving quickly because docs and SDK samples show both the new Responses based prompt-agent flow and the create_agent thread-run flow.

## Worked example

```python
# Run inside your application environment before copying SDK snippets.
from importlib.metadata import version, PackageNotFoundError

for package in ["azure-ai-projects", "azure-ai-agents", "azure-identity"]:
    try:
        print(package, version(package))
    except PackageNotFoundError:
        print(package, "not installed")

print("Rule: new Foundry quickstart code expects azure-ai-projects >= 2.0.0.")
print("Rule: thread run samples using create_agent come from azure-ai-agents sample APIs.")
```

*Print the installed packages and fail fast on mixed assumptions*

drills.q: Which package does the current Foundry quickstart tell Python learners to install?
What method creates a prompt-agent version in the current quickstart?
Why is this concept marked uncertain?
drills.a: Because Microsoft documentation and SDK samples expose both new Projects 2.x Responses APIs and azure-ai-agents thread-run samples during a transition.

teachItBack: Use the new Projects 2.x quickstart as the default path. Know that azure-ai-agents still documents thread, message, run, and run-step flows used by many samples. When copying code, match package version, endpoint format, and API family before debugging anything else.

glossary.definition: Python client for a Foundry project endpoint.
Current Projects 2.x model for defining a prompt agent.
Responses API extra body value that tells Foundry which agent to use.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Foundry quickstart with Azure AI Projects 2.x

quiz.q: Which Python package and API shape does the current Foundry quickstart emphasize for new projects?
choices: requests with a hand-written API key
quiz.explanation: The current quickstart installs azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 and creates prompt agent versions through AIProjectClient.
